Ever wondered why Amazon forces you to filter order history by year?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The answer comes down to core database architecture. Amazon relies heavily on Amazon DynamoDB (DDB) for its retail use-cases.
To understand the filter, we need to look at how DynamoDB handles data under the hood.
DynamoDB is a serverless NoSQL database. It indexes data using a Partition Key (PK) and an optional Sort Key (SK).

• The Partition Key is hashed to determine the physical machine where the data lives.

• The Sort Key physically organizes the data inside that partition.
To store an order, you might use the Customer ID as the PK, and “year#month#order_id” as the SK. Every order a customer places is stored in the same partition, neatly sorted by time.
Here is a quick look at the data structure:
DynamoDB requires you to know the exact Partition Key to fetch data efficiently. However, on the Sort Key, it allows conditional queries like “begins_with”.
Why can’t you just query everything at once without a year filter? Two reasons: Architecture and Payload Limits.
